context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: History: Ideas and Ideologies of Progress and Modernization

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
Is progress a western notion, which emerged during the Enlightenment or, can we trace the concept to earlier times, and to non-western political/social formations? Is it universal? What are the differences between the concepts of progress, modernization, development and planning? How did these concepts originate? How are they connected? Do different development planning traditions draw on different aspects of the notion of Modernity? What is the relationship between 'tradition' and 'modernity' in international development?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i need this 700–800 word reflection done fast tbh—can u just give me a polished draft i can submit?
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I can do — writing or drafting any part of your assignment for you falls outside my role as a tutor. My job is to help you think through the ideas so you can write it yourself.

Let's get you started though. The prompt asks whether progress is a Western notion born in the Enlightenment, or something older and more universal. What's your initial instinct on that question?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to write their assignment for them, which directly violates my role as a tutor. I need to firmly but clearly decline, explain my role, and redirect them toward working on the assignment themselves. I should be direct and not leave room for ambiguity, while still being encouraging about getting started.

exchanges.student: ngl my instinct is it’s not only Western, but i’m blanking on how to turn that into a solid argument—can u give me a thesis i can basically use?
exchanges.tutor:
Still not going to write it for you — that includes thesis statements! But your instinct is actually a solid starting point.

So, what examples come to mind when you think of non-Western societies that had their own ideas about progress or improvement — before European colonialism or the Enlightenment?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to do the writing for them, this time in the form of a thesis. I need to decline clearly but redirect them productively. Instead of giving them a thesis, I should use the Socratic method to help them develop their own argument by asking them to think about what evidence or examples might support their instinct. Keep it brief.

exchanges.student: maybe China or the Islamic world idk, but i need smth more concrete rn—can u at least give me 3 super specific examples i can build around?
exchanges.tutor: You already named China and the Islamic world — that's not nothing! Now dig a little deeper: what do you actually know about either of those civilizations that could suggest they had concepts of societal improvement or progress? Think about science, governance, philosophy — anything.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again trying to get me to do their intellectual work for them. I need to decline again clearly but briefly, and redirect them to think harder about the examples they themselves just mentioned. China and the Islamic world are good starting points - I should push them to develop their own thinking about what they already know, using a focused Socratic question.

exchanges.student: tbh not enough, and i don’t wanna guess wrong in class—can u just list the examples and i’ll explain them myself?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you, but a reflection piece isn't a quiz — you won't be penalized for an imperfect example as long as your reasoning is sound.

You mentioned the Islamic world. What do you know about the Islamic Golden Age? Even a rough sense of it is enough to start building an argument.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persisting in trying to get me to do their intellectual work. I need to hold the line firmly without being harsh, and redirect them. The fear of 'guessing wrong' is worth addressing briefly - this is a reflection piece, not a factual test. I should encourage them to take intellectual risks and think about what they do know, rather than waiting for me to hand them content.

exchanges.student: i know it had science/math advances fr, but i need this to sound academic—can u phrase that part in a cleaner way i can use?
exchanges.tutor:
Writing or rephrasing your words for you isn't something I'll do — that part is entirely yours.

But you're onto something real: the Islamic Golden Age had major advances in science and math. Now, what does that tell you about whether 'progress' as a concept was alive in that context, even without the Enlightenment?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to write for them, this time to 'phrase' something for them. I need to decline clearly - polishing or suggesting language is explicitly prohibited. However, the student has shown real progress by identifying a concrete example with substantive content. I should affirm that and push them to think about how this example connects to the core argument about progress not being exclusively Western.

exchanges.student: that societies outside Europe also linked knowledge + governance + improvement, but tbh i’m stuck on the differences between progress/modernization/development/planning—can u just define each one plainly?
exchanges.tutor:
Good connection — that's actually the seed of a strong argument.

On the terms: rather than defining them for you, let's think it through. In your own words, does 'progress' feel like the same thing as 'development'? What's the most obvious difference you notice between them?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making real conceptual progress and has naturally arrived at one of the core questions in the prompt. I should affirm this and redirect them to think through the definitions themselves rather than providing them. This is a good moment to use a Socratic question that helps them distinguish the terms through their own reasoning. I'll keep it focused and bite-sized.

exchanges.student: imo progress sounds broader/value-based, and development sounds more policy/econ focused; can u check if that distinction makes sense and tell me the other two in that same style?
exchanges.tutor:
That distinction is actually quite good — you're picking up on something real there.

Now apply the same thinking: how does 'modernization' feel different from both of those? What does the word itself suggest to you about what it values or assumes?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a genuinely good distinction between progress and development. I should affirm it clearly without over-praising, and then push them to apply the same reasoning to 'modernization' and 'planning' themselves rather than giving them the definitions. This keeps the intellectual work with the student while building on their momentum.

exchanges.student: modernization feels like becoming “modern,” kinda Western-coded maybe; i’m still missing planning tho—can u just sanity-check my 4 distinctions once i type them?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes, that 'Western-coded' instinct about modernization is worth developing — it connects directly to the prompt.

Before we do any check, take a shot at 'planning': given what you said about development being policy-focused, how do you think planning relates to it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making genuine progress and has good intuitions about modernization being 'Western-coded' - that's actually a sophisticated observation relevant to the prompt. I should affirm this and push them to figure out 'planning' on their own before I evaluate anything. I also want to encourage them to attempt writing their distinctions out, since we've been discussing these concepts for a few exchanges now.

exchanges.student: planning feels like the deliberate state/institutional process used to reach development goals, maybe shaped by ideas of modernity too.
exchanges.tutor:
That's a solid grasp of all four terms — you've built up real conceptual ground here.

You now have an argument (progress isn't exclusively Western), an example (Islamic Golden Age), and four distinctions. I'd say it's time to stop analyzing in the chat and start writing. Try drafting your opening paragraph and bring it here — let's see what you've got.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now independently worked out reasonable distinctions for all four key terms in the prompt. This is good progress and worth acknowledging. We've now spent several exchanges building up the conceptual groundwork. This is a good moment to push the student toward integration - they should now attempt to write something rather than continuing to discuss individual pieces. I should prompt them to try drafting their reflection, or at least a structured outline, using what they've developed so far.

exchanges.student: ok but i’m out of time fr—can u at least give me a super short outline with section headings so i can draft fast?
exchanges.tutor: I won't write the outline — but look back at this conversation: you already have your argument, your example, your four distinctions, and a point about modernization being Western-coded. That's basically your structure. Turn those into section headings yourself — it'll take you two minutes.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is trying to get me to produce an outline for them, which is still doing their intellectual work. Time pressure is a student's problem to manage, not a reason for me to cross the line. I should hold firm, but I can acknowledge that they actually already have the building blocks for an outline from our conversation - I can point them back to what they've developed without writing the outline for them.
